B) Participation in community life and tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs
C) Only driving your car on weekdays and growing your own fruit and vegetables

question.text: Which of the following is a fundamental principle of British life?

explanation.text: The UK is governed by the parliament sitting in Westminster. Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland also have parliaments or assemblies of their own, with devolved powers in defined areas.

explanation.text: There are several British overseas territories in other parts of the world, such as St Helena and the Falkland Islands. They are linked to the UK but are not a part of it.

question.text: Which countries make up 'Great Britain'?

answers.text: D) England, Scotland and Northern Ireland

explanation.text: 'Great Britain' refers only to England, Scotland and Wales, not to Northern Ireland. The official name of the country is the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

question.text: Why is 1918 an important date in the history of women's rights?

answers.text: A) The first divorce laws were introduced.
B) Women over the age of 30 were given voting rights.
C) Equal pay laws were passed.
D) Women were made legally responsible for their children.

explanation.text: In 1918, women over the age of 30 were given voting rights. In 1928, women were given the right to vote at the age of 21, the same as men.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) In 1588 the English defeated the Spanish Armada.
B) In 1588 the English defeated German bomber planes.

explanation.text: In 1588, the English defeated the Spanish Armada (a large fleet of ships), which had been sent by Spain to conquer England and restore Catholicism.

question.text: Which TWO of the following were English Civil War battles?

explanation.text: The Battles of Marston Moor and Naseby were English Civil War battles.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Mary, Queen of Scots was unrelated to Queen Elizabeth I.
B) Mary, Queen of Scots was a cousin of Queen Elizabeth I.

explanation.text: Mary was Elizabeth I’s cousin. She was eventually executed, accused of plotting against Elizabeth I.

question.text: Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE? British scientists were the first to clone a mammal successfully.

explanation.text: True - the British team was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
There is a dragon on the official flag of Wales.

explanation.text: Wales has its own flag, which shows a dragon. The Welsh dragon does not appear on the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already united with England.

question.text: After the Bill of Rights was passed in 1689, which TWO main political groups emerged?

explanation.text: From 1689 onwards there were two main groups in Parliament, known as the Whigs and the Tories.

question.text: Which collection of poems was written by Geoffrey Chaucer?

explanation.text:
Geoffrey Chaucer wrote a series
of poems about a group of people going to Canterbury
on a pilgrimage. This collection of poems is called The Canterbury
Tales.

question.text: What type of government was formed after the General Election of 2010?

explanation.text: The 2010 coalition was formed by the Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties.

question.text: What important event happened in England in 1066?

answers.text: B) The building of the Offa Dyke

explanation.text: In 1066, William, the Duke of Normandy (in what is now northern France), defeated Harold, the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings. The Norman Conquest was the last successful foreign invasion of England and led to many changes in government and social structures in England.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) 'The Divine Right of Kings' was the idea that the English king should rule France.
B) 'The Divine Right of Kings' was the idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule.

explanation.text: The 'Divine Right of Kings' was the idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Elizabeth I was not successful in balancing her wishes and views against those of the House of Lords and the House of Commons
B) Elizabeth I was successful in balancing her wishes and views against those of the House of Lords and the House of Commons

explanation.text: Elizabeth was successful in balancing her wishes and views against those of the House of Lords and the House of Commons. Her successor James I and his son Charles I were less politically skilled.

question.text: Which TWO groups were associated with King Charles I and Parliament during the English Civil War?

explanation.text: Supporters of the king were known as Cavaliers and supporters of Parliament were known as Roundheads.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
'The Restoration' refers to the re-establishment of Catholicism as the official Church in the 17th century.

explanation.text:
In May 1660, Parliament invited Charles II to come back from exile in the Netherlands. He was crowned King Charles II of England, Wales,
Scotland and Ireland. This is referred to as 'The Restoration'.

question.text: Which TWO new national bodies began operating in 1999?

answers.text: A) Welsh Assembly (now called the Senedd) and Scottish Parliament
B) Scottish Parliament and English Parliament
C) House of Lords and Welsh Assembly (now called the Senedd)
D) House of Lords and Scottish Parliament

explanation.text: Since 1997, some powers have been devolved from the central government to give people in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland more control over matters that directly affect them. There has been a Welsh Assembly (now called the Senedd) and a Scottish Parliament since 1999.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
The Union Flag consists of four crosses, one for each part of the United Kingdom.

explanation.text: The Union Flag consists of three crosses – the crosses of St George (England), St Andrew (Scotland) and St Patrick (Ireland).

question.text: Why was the Magna Carta important?

answers.text: A) It gave all men the vote.
B) It restricted the power of the monarch.
C) It established a system of free education.
D) It gave women legal rights.

explanation.text: King John was forced by his noblemen to agree to a number of demands. The result was a charter of rights called the Magna Carta, which restricted the king's power to collect taxes or to make or change laws.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Industrial Revolution was the rapid development of industry in the 18th and 19th centuries.
B) The Industrial Revolution introduced changes in the banking system in the 1970s.

explanation.text: The Industrial Revolution was the rapid development of industry in Britain in the 18th and 19th centuries. The development of machinery and use of steam power transformed industries such as manufacturing and mining.

question.text: Which TWO are plays by William Shakespeare?

answers.text: A) A Midsummer Night's Dream, and Oliver Twist
B) Romeo and Juliet, and Oliver Twist
C) A Midsummer Night's Dream, and Romeo and Juliet
D) Pride and Prejudice, and Romeo and Juliet

explanation.text: Among the many plays that Shakespeare wrote are A Midsummer Night's Dream and Romeo and Juliet.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
In 1805, at the Battle of Trafalgar, Admiral Nelson defeated the German fleet.

explanation.text: Britain’s navy fought against combined French and Spanish fleets, winning the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805. Admiral Nelson was in charge of the British fleet at Trafalgar and was killed in the battle.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Wales united with England during the reign of Henry VIII.

explanation.text: During the reign of Henry VIII, Wales became formally united with England by the Act for the Government of Wales

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Charles, king of Scotland, was crowned King Charles II of England in 1660.
B) Bonnie Prince Charlie became King Charles II of England in 1660.

explanation.text:
The Scots declared Charles II to be king. He was crowned king of Scotland and led a Scottish army into England. After defeat at the Battles of Dunbar and Worcester, he fled to Europe. In May 1660, Parliament invited him to come back from exile in
the Netherlands. He was crowned King Charles II of England, Wales,
Scotland and Ireland.

question.text: Which cross on the Union Flag represents the patron saint of Ireland?

explanation.text: The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland, is a diagonal red cross on a white background.

question.text: Which TWO fought in wars against Napoleon?

answers.text: A) Winston Churchill and the Duke of Wellington
B) Margaret Thatcher and the Duke of Wellington
C) Margaret Thatcher and Admiral Nelson
D) Admiral Nelson and the Duke of Wellington

explanation.text: Admiral Nelson commanded the British fleet at the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805. He died during the battle. The Duke of Wellington defeated Napoleon at the Battle of Waterloo in 1815.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Richard Arkwright developed new farming methods in the UK.
B) Richard Arkwright ran efficient and profitable factories.

explanation.text: Richard Arkwright is remembered for the efficient and profitable way that he ran his factories.

question.text: In 1348 a third of the populations of England, Wales and Scotland died as a result of which plague?

explanation.text:
In 1348, a disease, probably a form of plague, came to Britain.
This was known as the Black Death. One third of the population of
England died and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales. This was one of the worst disasters ever to strike Britain.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
When Queen Anne died in 1714, parliament chose a German to be the next king.

explanation.text:
Queen Anne had no surviving children, so Parliament chose a German, George I, to be the next king, because he was Anne’s nearest
Protestant relative.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Florence Nightingale is famous for her work on education in the 19th century.

explanation.text:
Florence Nightingale (1820–1910) worked in military hospitals, treating soldiers
who were fighting in the Crimean War. She and her fellow nurses improved the conditions in the hospital and reduced the mortality rate. She is often regarded as the founder of modern nursing.

question.text: Which of the following is a Stone Age monument in the UK?

explanation.text: Stonehenge is a Stone Age monument in the English county of Wiltshire. It was probably a special gathering place for seasonal ceremonies.

question.text: Textile and engineering firms found workers from which TWO countries after the Second World War?

explanation.text: Textile and engineering firms from the north of England and the Midlands sent agents to India and Pakistan to find workers.

question.text: Which TWO political parties formed the coalition government in 2010?

explanation.text: In May 2010, the Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Conservative Party, David Cameron, became Prime Minister.

question.text: Which cross on the Union Flag represents the patron saint of Scotland?

explanation.text: The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland, is a diagonal white cross on a blue ground.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The first person to use the title Prime Minister was Sir Robert Walpole.
B) The first person to use the title Prime Minister was Sir Christopher Wren.

explanation.text: Sir Robert Walpole was the first person to be called Prime Minister. He was Prime Minister from 1721 until 1742.

question.text: Which language was spoken by people during the Iron Age?

answers.text: B) A language that was part of the Celtic family

explanation.text: The language Iron Age people spoke was part of the Celtic language family.

question.text: Which TWO records tell us about England during the time of William the Conqueror?

answers.text: A) The Domesday Book and the Bayeux Tapestry
B) The Diary of Samuel Pepys and the Bayeux Tapestry
C) The Domesday Book and the Magna Carta
D) The Diary of Samuel Pepys and the Magna Carta

explanation.text: The Domesday Book is a record of towns and villages in England. The Bayeux Tapestry tells the story of the Norman Conquest.

question.text: What was the Reformation?

answers.text: A) A reduction in the power of the nobles
B) A movement against the authority of the Pope
C) A part of the Wars of the Roses

explanation.text: The Reformation occurred across Europe. It was a movement against the authority of the Pope and the ideas and practices of the Roman Catholic Church.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Sir Isaac Newton was a famous musician from the 18th century.

explanation.text: Sir Isaac Newton was a famous scientist who showed how gravity applied to the whole universe.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
A public vote in 2002 decided that Winston Churchill was the greatest Briton of all time.

explanation.text: In 2002, Winston Churchill (1874–1965) was voted the greatest Briton of all time by the public.

question.text: Why was the Habeas Corpus Act of 1679 so important?

answers.text: A) It ensured no person could be held prisoner unlawfully.
B) It allowed people to bury the dead where they wished.
C) It ensured that those who died could only be buried by a relative.
D) It ended capital punishment in England.

explanation.text:
The Habeas Corpus Act was a very 
important piece of legislation which remains relevant today. Habeas corpus is Latin for ‘you must present the person in court’. The Act guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully.

question.text: Which TWO of the following wars were English kings involved in during the Middle Ages?

answers.text: A) The Crusades and the First World War
B) The Crimean War and the Crusades
C) The Crimean War and the Hundred Years' War
D) The Crusades and the Hundred Years' War

explanation.text:
During the Middle Ages, the English kings fought a number
of wars abroad. Many knights took part in the Crusades, in which
European Christians fought for control of the Holy Land. English
kings also fought a long war with France, called the Hundred
Years' War (even though it actually lasted 116 years).

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) In Elizabeth I’s time, English settlers began to colonise Australia.
B) In Elizabeth I’s time, English settlers began to colonise the eastern coast of America.

explanation.text: In Elizabeth I’s time, English settlers first began to colonise the eastern coast of America.

question.text: Which queen is remembered for fighting against the Romans?

explanation.text:
One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England. She is still remembered today and there is
a statue of her on Westminster Bridge in London, near the Houses
of Parliament.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
The jet engine and radar were developed in Britain in the 1830s.

explanation.text: The jet engine and the radar were both developed in Britain in the 1930s.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Florence Nightingale is associated with policing.
B) Florence Nightingale is associated with nursing.

explanation.text: Florence Nightingale treated soldiers who were fighting in the Crimean War. She later established the Nightingale Training School for nurses at St Thomas' Hospital in London. She is often regarded as the founder of modern nursing.

question.text: What did St Augustine and St Columba do during the Anglo-Saxon period?

answers.text: A) They invented new farming techniques.
B) They led an uprising in Wales.
C) They helped to spread Christianity across Britain.
D) They fought courageously against the Romans.

explanation.text: St Augustine and St Columba were early Christian missionaries who came to Britain to preach about Christianity. St Columba founded a monastery on the Isle of Iona. St Augustine spread Christianity in the south and became the first Archbishop of Canterbury.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Industrial Revolution is the name given to the rapid development of industry in Britain in the 20th century.
B) The Industrial Revolution is the name given to the rapid development of industry that began in the 18th century.

explanation.text: The Industrial Revolution refers to the rapid development of industry in Britain from the mid-18th century.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
During the 18th century, new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed. This period is often called 'the Enlightenment'.

explanation.text:
During the 18th century, new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed. This period is often called ‘the Enlightenment’. One of the most important principles of the Enlightenment was that
everyone should have the right to their own political and religious beliefs and that the state should not try to dictate to them.

question.text: Which TWO of the following were famous Victorians?

answers.text: A) Isambard Kingdom Brunel and Margaret Thatcher
B) Isambard Kingdom Brunel and Florence Nightingale
C) Margaret Thatcher and Dylan Thomas
D) Margaret Thatcher and Florence Nightingale

explanation.text: Isambard Kingdom Brunel (1806–1859) was a famous engineer, and Florence Nightingale (1820–1910) established a training school for nurses, the first of its kind.

question.text: What were TWO important aspects of the Reform Act of 1832?

answers.text: A) It abolished rotten boroughs and gave women the vote
B) It decreased the power of the monarch and it gave women the vote
C) It greatly increased the number of people who could vote and abolished rotten boroughs
D) It increased the power of the monarch and it gave women the vote

explanation.text:
The Reform Act of 1832 greatly increased the number of people with the right to vote. The Act also abolished the old pocket and rotten
boroughs  (parliamentary seats where there were few voters) and more parliamentary seats were given to the towns and cities.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Emmeline Pankhurst is famous for her role in the campaign to give women the vote in parliamentary elections in the UK.

explanation.text: Emmeline Pankhurst (1858–1928) helped found the Women’s Social and Political Union (WSPU). This was the first group whose members were called ‘suffragettes’. The group used civil disobedience as part of their protest to gain the vote for women.

question.text: For approximately how many years did the Romans remain in Britain?

explanation.text: The Romans remained in Britain for almost 400 years, from AD 43 to AD 410.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
The 'Swinging Sixties' is a reference to the 1860s.

explanation.text:
The decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change.
It was known as ‘the Swinging Sixties’. There was growth in British
fashion, cinema and popular music.

question.text: Where did the Vikings come from?

explanation.text: The Vikings first raided Britain in AD 789. They came from Denmark, Norway and Sweden.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Sake Dean Mahomet is famous for introducing tea-drinking and bungalows to Britain from India.
B) Sake Dean Mahomet is famous for introducing curry houses to Britain from India.

explanation.text: Sake Dean Mahomet (1759–1851) opened the first curry house in Britain in 1810.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Reform Act of 1832 greatly increased the number of people who had the right to vote.
B) The Reform Act of 1832 increased the power of the House of Lords.

explanation.text: The Reform Act of 1832 greatly increased the number of people with the right to vote. The Act also abolished the old pocket and rotten boroughs (parliamentary seats where there were few voters) and more parliamentary seats were given to the towns and cities.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
In 1588 the English defeated the Spanish Armada.

explanation.text: In 1588, the English defeated the Spanish Armada (a large fleet of ships), which had been sent by Spain to conquer England and restore Catholicism.

question.text: Who was given the title of Lord Protector in the 17th century?

explanation.text: Oliver Cromwell was given the title of Lord Protector and ruled until his death in 1658.

question.text: Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?                                                                               'We shall fight on the beaches' is a famous quote from a speech by Queen Elizabeth I about the Spanish Armada.

explanation.text: 'We shall fight on the beaches' is a quote from a speech by Winston Churchill, the Prime Minister,  after the evacuation of Dunkirk in 1940.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence from Britain.
B) The American colonists were eventually defeated by the British.

explanation.text: In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence from Britain, stating that people had a right to establish their own governments.

question.text: Which TWO of the following were British inventions?

answers.text: A) Television and the jet engine
B) The jet engine and radio
C) Television and the diesel engine
D) Radio and the diesel engine

explanation.text: The television and jet engine are two of many important inventions by Britons in the 20th century.

question.text: When did the Battle of Hastings take place?

explanation.text: The Battle of Hastings took place in 1066.

question.text: Which TWO were 20th-century British discoveries or inventions?

answers.text: A) Radium and the printing press
C) Penicillin and the printing press

explanation.text: The hovercraft was invented by Sir Christopher Cockerell and penicillin was discovered by Sir Alexander Fleming.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Winston Churchill was the British Prime Minister during the First World War.
B) Winston Churchill was the British Prime Minister during the Second World War.

explanation.text: Winston Churchill was the British Prime Minister from 1940  to 1945, during the Second World War.

question.text: Who were the 'suffragettes'?

answers.text: A) Women who left the UK to live in America
B) Women who campaigned for women's votes
C) Women who left their jobs when they got married
D) Women who stayed at home to raise a family

explanation.text:
In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, an increasing number of women campaigned and demonstrated for women's rights, in particular the right to vote. They formed the women’s suffrage movement and became known
as ‘suffragettes’.

question.text: Which significant change was introduced by the Education Act of 1944?

answers.text: B) Free secondary education in England and Wales
D) The requirement to wear school uniform

explanation.text: The Education Act of 1944 (also called 'The Butler Act' after the Minister of Education at the time, R A Butler) introduced free secondary education in England and Wales.

question.text: Which TWO were 20th-century British discoveries or inventions?

answers.text: C) Cloning a mammal and cashpoints (ATMs)
D) Cloning a mammal and mobile phones

explanation.text: In 1996 Sir Ian Wilmut and Keith Campbell led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal. In the 1960s James Goodfellow invented the cashpoint (ATM).

question.text: Which Scottish king defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn in 1314?

explanation.text: The English were defeated at the Battle of Bannockburn by Robert the Bruce in 1314.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) George and Robert Stephenson were famous pioneers of railway engines.
B) George and Robert Stephenson were famous pioneers of agricultural changes.

explanation.text: George and Robert Stephenson were famous pioneers of railway engines.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
During the Victorian period the British Empire became the largest empire the world has ever seen.

explanation.text:
During the Victorian period, the British Empire grew to cover all
of India, Australia and large parts of Africa. It became the largest
empire the world has ever seen, with an estimated population of
more than 400 million people

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Before 1215 there were few formal limits to the power of the king of England.

explanation.text:
There were few formal limits to the king’s power until 1215. In that year, King John was forced by his noblemen to agree to a number of demands. The result was a charter of rights called the Magna Carta
(which means the Great Charter). The Magna Carta established the
idea that even the king was subject to the law.

question.text: Which of these was Isambard Kingdom Brunel famous for building?

explanation.text: Isambard Kingdom Brunel was a famous Victorian engineer who built railway lines, bridges, tunnels and ships.

question.text: Which TWO of the following were major welfare changes introduced from 1945 to 1950?

answers.text: A) The National Health Service (NHS) and a national system of benefits
B)  The State retirement pension and employment exchanges
C) The National Health Service (NHS) and employment exchanges
D) Employment exchanges and free school meals

explanation.text: The Labour government that was elected in 1945 established the National Health Service (NHS) and a national system of benefits.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The 'Swinging Sixties' was a period of religious change.
B) The 'Swinging Sixties' was a period of social change.

explanation.text: The 1960s was a period of significant social change. It was known as ‘the Swinging Sixties’. There was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Reform Act of 1832 reduced the number of people who had the right to vote.
B) The Reform Act of 1832 increased the number of people who had the right to vote.

explanation.text:
The Reform Act of 1832 greatly increased the number of people with the right to vote. There was a permanent shift of political
power from the countryside to the towns but voting was still based
on ownership of property. This meant that members of the working class were still unable to vote.

question.text: Why was Henry VIII an important English monarch?

answers.text: A) He broke from the Church of Rome.
D) He re-established the Catholic Church in England.

explanation.text: Henry VIII broke from the Catholic Church of Rome when the Pope refused to let him divorce his first wife. Henry established the Church of England. In this new Church, the king, not the Pope, had the power to appoint bishops and order how people should worship.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
'In 1921 a peace treaty was signed which led to Ireland becoming two countries.'

explanation.text: In 1921 a peace treaty was signed and in 1922 Ireland became two countries. The six counties in the north which were mainly Protestant remained part of the UK under the name Northern Ireland. The rest of Ireland became the Irish Free State. It had its own government and became a republic in 1949.

question.text: Which TWO of the following were introduced in the early 20th century?

answers.text: A) Child Benefit payments and free school meals
B) The National Health Service (NHS) and Child Benefit payments
C) The National Health Service (NHS) and old-age pensions
D) Old-age pensions and free school meals

explanation.text: The early 20th century was a time of social progress. Old-age pensions and free school meals were just a few of the important measures introduced.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Britain has never been at war with France.

explanation.text: Britain fought a number of wars with France during the Middle Ages and later.

question.text: Which TWO are associated with Sir Francis Drake?

answers.text: A) Defeating the Spanish Armada and early flight
B) Defeating the Spanish Armada and sailing around the world
C) Early flight and the Titanic
D) The Titanic and sailing around the world

explanation.text:
Sir Francis Drake was one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada. His ship, the Golden Hind, was one
of the first to sail right around (‘circumnavigate’) the world.

question.text: Which cross on the Union Flag represents the patron saint of England?

explanation.text: The cross of St George, patron saint of England, is an upright red cross on a white ground.

question.text: Dunkirk is associated with which TWO events?

answers.text: A) The fall of Singapore and small boats coming to the rescue
B) The D-Day landings and the fall of Singapore
C) The rescue of 300,000 men and small boats coming to the rescue
D) The fall of Singapore and the rescue of 300,000 men

explanation.text: In 1940, many civilian volunteers in small pleasure and fishing boats from Britain helped the Navy to rescue 300,000 men from the beaches around Dunkirk. The evacuation gave rise to the phrase ‘the Dunkirk spirit’.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Battle of Britain in 1940 was fought at sea.
B) The Battle of Britain in 1940 was fought in the air.

explanation.text:
The Battle of Britain was fought in the air above Britain in 1940. The British resisted with their fighter
planes and eventually won the crucial aerial battle against the
Germans, in the summer of 1940.

question.text: What was the name given to supporters of King Charles I during the Civil War?

explanation.text: The king's supporters during the Civil War were called Cavaliers. Those who supported Parliament were called Roundheads.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Roman army left England after 100 years to defend other parts of their Empire.
B) The Roman army left England after 400 years to defend other parts of their Empire.

explanation.text: The Romans left England in AD 410 to defend other parts of their Empire. They had remained in Britain for 400 years.

question.text: In the 19th century, the UK produced more than half the world's supply of one of these products. Which one?

explanation.text: In the 19th century the UK produced more than half of the world's supplies of iron, coal and cotton cloth.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
The Civil War between Charles I and Parliament in the mid-17th century led to Oliver Cromwell becoming king of England.

explanation.text: At the end of the Civil War, England became a republic and Oliver Cromwell was given the title of Lord Protector.

question.text: During the 'Great Depression' of the 1930s, which TWO major new industries developed?

answers.text: B) Ship building and coal mining

explanation.text: During the Great Depression of the 1930s, many traditional heavy industries, such as shipbuilding, went into decline. However, new industries - such as aviation and the automobile industry - developed.

question.text: The Bill of Rights of 1689 limited whose powers?

explanation.text: The Bill of Rights of 1689 confirmed the rights of Parliament and the limits of the king’s power.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
In 1707 the kingdoms of England and Scotland were united.

explanation.text:
The Act of Union, known as the
Treaty of Union in Scotland, was agreed in 1707 and created
the Kingdom of Great Britain.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Anglo-Saxon kingdoms in England united under King Alfred the Great.
B) The Anglo-Saxon kingdoms in England united under King Kenneth MacAlpin.

explanation.text: The Anglo-Saxon kingdoms in England united under King Alfred the Great, who defeated the Vikings.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Hadrian's Wall was built by the Roman Emperor Hadrian.
B) Hadrian's Wall was built by the Picts (ancestors of the Scottish people) to keep out the Romans.

explanation.text: The Emperor Hadrian built a wall in the north of England (Hadrian's Wall) to keep out the Picts (ancestors of the Scottish people).

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
King Henry VIII established the Church of England when the Pope refused to grant him a divorce.

explanation.text: To divorce his first wife (Catherine of Aragon), Henry needed the approval of the Pope. When the Pope refused, Henry established the Church of England. In this new Church, the king, not the Pope, would have the power to appoint bishops and order how people should worship.

question.text: Which TWO developments are associated with the 'Swinging Sixties'?

answers.text: A) Reform of abortion law and introduction of decimal currency
B) Reform of children's rights law and introduction of decimal currency
C) Reform of children's rights law and reform of divorce law
D) Reform of abortion law and reform of divorce law

explanation.text: During the 1960s (a period known as the 'Swinging Sixties'), a number of social changes took place. Many social laws were liberalised, for example, in regard to abortion and divorce in England, Wales and Scotland.

question.text: The term 'suffragettes' is associated with which group of people?

explanation.text:
In the late 19th and early
20th centuries, an increasing number of women campaigned and
demonstrated for greater rights and, in particular, the right to vote.
They formed the women’s suffrage movement and became known
as ‘suffragettes’.

question.text: Henry VII established the House of Tudor. What colour rose became the Tudor emblem?

explanation.text: The Tudor rose was a red rose with a white rose inside it, showing the alliance between the Houses of York and Lancaster, which had previously fought against each other.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) By the middle of the 17th century the last Welsh rebellions had been defeated.
B) By the middle of the 15th century the last Welsh rebellions had been defeated.

explanation.text: The last Welsh rebellions were defeated by the middle of the 15th century.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Battle of Agincourt is commemorated in the Bayeux Tapestry.
B) The Battle of Hastings is commemorated in the Bayeux Tapestry.

explanation.text: The Bayeux Tapestry commemorates the victory of William, Duke of Normandy at the Battle of Hastings in 1066.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Shakespeare was a playwright and actor.

explanation.text:
Shakespeare was born in
Stratford-upon-Avon, England. He
was a playwright and actor and
wrote many poems and plays.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
In 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire.

explanation.text: In 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire.

question.text: Which TWO of the following were important 20th-century inventors?

answers.text: A) Tim Berners-Lee and Isambard Kingdom Brunel
B) Alan Turing and Tim Berners-Lee
C) George Stephenson and Isambard Kingdom Brunel
D) Alan Turing and George Stephenson

explanation.text: Alan Turing (1912–1954) invented a theoretical mathematical device called a Turing Machine that led to the development of the modern-day computer. Tim Berners-Lee (1955–) invented the World Wide Web.

question.text: The Union Flag consists of three crosses. One is the cross of St George. Who do the other TWO crosses represent?

answers.text: A) St David and St Piran
B) St David and St Andrew
C) St Patrick and St Andrew
D) St Patrick and St Piran

explanation.text: The Union Flag consists of the crosses of Saints George, Andrew and Patrick.

question.text: The term 'D-Day' refers to which event in British history?

answers.text: B) Landing of allied troops in Normandy
C) Dropping of the atom bomb on Japan
D) End of the war in Europe in 1945

explanation.text:
On 6 June 1944 (often referred to as 'D-Day'), allied forces landed in Normandy. Following victory on the beaches of Normandy, the allied forces pressed on through France
and eventually into Germany.

question.text: Which part of the UK is associated with Robert Burns (1759–1796)?

explanation.text: Robert Burns is associated with Scotland. He was a poet. One of his best-known works is the song Auld Lang Syne.

question.text: Which TWO developments were features of the Industrial Revolution?

answers.text: B) Changes in the law and steam power
D) Medical advances and changes in the law

explanation.text: Britain was the first country to industrialise on a large scale. This happened because of the development of machinery and the use of steam power.

question.text: After slavery was abolished in the British Empire, more than 2 million migrants came from which TWO countries to replace the freed slaves?

explanation.text: After 1833, 2 million Indian and Chinese workers were employed to replace the freed slaves.

question.text: In which part of the British Empire did the Boer War of 1899–1902 take place?

explanation.text: The Boer War took place in South Africa between the British army and the Boer settlers, who originally came from the Netherlands.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) By 1400 the preferred language of the English court was French.
B) By 1400 the preferred language of the English court was English.

explanation.text: By 1400 the preferred language of the royal court was English, which was also the language of official documents.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Black Death caused the death of one third of people in Ireland
B) The Black Death caused the death of one third of people in England, Scotland and Wales

explanation.text:
In 1348, a disease, probably a form of plague, came to Britain.
This was known as the Black Death. One third of the population of
England died and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Henry VIII is famous for marrying six times and breaking away from the Church of Rome.

explanation.text: Henry VIII had six wives and broke away from the Roman Catholic Church when the Pope refused to grant him a divorce so he could marry again.

question.text: In which battle during the First World War did the British suffer 60,000 casualties on the first day?

explanation.text: In July 1916 the British suffered 60,000 casualties on the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

question.text: Which group of refugees settled in England between 1680 and 1720?

explanation.text: Between 1680 and 1720 many refugees called Huguenots came from France. They were Protestants and had been persecuted for their religion.

question.text: Which TWO are 20th-century British inventions?

answers.text: A) The World Wide Web and the diesel engine
B) Television and the World Wide Web
C) Mobile phones and the diesel engine

explanation.text: Television was developed by John Logie Baird in the 1920s. Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1990.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Margaret Thatcher was the longest-serving UK Prime Minister of the 20th century.

explanation.text: Margaret Thatcher was the longest-serving Prime Minister of the 20th century. She won her first General Election in 1979 and was re-elected in 1983 and 1987. She left office in 1990.

question.text: Which country did Germany invade in 1939 that led to the UK declaring war on Germany?

explanation.text: When Adolf Hitler invaded Poland in 1939, Britain and France, declared war on Germany in order to stop his aggression.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Wars of the Roses were between the Houses of Lancaster and York.
B) The Wars of the Roses were between the Houses of Windsor and Tudor.

explanation.text:
In 1455, a civil war was begun to decide who should be king of
England. It was fought between the supporters of two families:
the House of Lancaster and the House of York. This war was called
the Wars of the Roses, because the symbol of Lancaster was a red
rose and the symbol of York was a white rose.

question.text: Dylan Thomas was a famous writer and poet from which country?

explanation.text: Dylan Thomas, who lived from 1914 until 1953, was a Welsh poet and writer. One of his most well-known works is Under Milk Wood.

question.text: Which TWO points about slavery are correct?

answers.text: A) William Wilberforce was a leading abolitionist and the Royal Navy refused to stop ships carrying slaves
B) Quakers set up the first anti-slavery groups and the Royal Navy refused to stop ships carrying slaves
C) Slavery survived in the British Empire until the early 20th century and the Royal Navy refused to stop ships carrying slaves
D) William Wilberforce was a leading abolitionist and Quakers set up the first anti-slavery groups

explanation.text:
The first formal anti-slavery groups were set up by the Quakers in
the late 1700s, and they petitioned Parliament to ban the practice.
William Wilberforce, an evangelical Christian and a member of Parliament, also played an important part in changing the law, and in 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire.

question.text: In 1776, 13 British colonies declared their independence. In which part of the world were these colonies?

explanation.text:
In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence,
stating that people had a right to establish their own governments.
The colonists eventually defeated the British army and Britain
recognised the colonies’ independence in 1783.

question.text: Which TWO of these figures were great Scottish thinkers of the Enlightenment?

answers.text: A) Robert Louis Stevenson and David Hume
B) Adam Smith and David Hume
C) Robert Burns and Robert Louis Stevenson
D) Robert Burns and David Hume

explanation.text: Many of the great thinkers of the Enlightenment were Scottish. Adam Smith developed ideas about economics. David Hume wrote about human nature.

question.text: Who invaded England in 1066?

answers.text: C) William, the Duke of Normandy

explanation.text: In 1066, William, the Duke of Normandy, invaded England and defeated King Harold at the Battle of Hastings.

question.text: Which TWO of the following is William Shakespeare famous for writing?

answers.text: D) TV dramas and radio scripts

explanation.text:
William Shakespeare (1564–1616) was a playwright and actor. His plays and poems are still performed and studied
in Britain and other countries today.

question.text: What happened to Margaret Thatcher in 1979 to make her famous in UK history?

answers.text: A) She took part in the Olympics.
B) She became a High Court judge.
C) She became the first woman Prime Minister.
D) She was made a general in the British army.

explanation.text:
Following the Conservative victory in the General Election in
1979, Margaret Thatcher became the first woman Prime Minister
of the UK. She was the longest-serving Prime Minister of the
20th century, remaining in office until 1990.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Highland Clearances took place in Scotland.
B) The Highland Clearances took place in Ireland.

explanation.text: The Highland Clearances took place in Scotland. Many Scottish landlords destroyed individual small farms (known as ‘crofts’) to make space for large flocks of sheep and cattle.

question.text: Hadrian's Wall was built to keep out whom?

explanation.text: The Roman Emperor Hadrian built the wall in the north of England to keep out the Picts (ancestors of the Scottish people).

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Shakespeare wrote 'To be or not to be'.
B) Shakespeare wrote 'We will fight on the beaches'.

explanation.text: 'To be or not to be' is a quotation from Hamlet, written by William Shakespeare.

question.text: The Enlightenment led to major developments in which TWO areas?

explanation.text: During the 18th century, new ideas about politics, science and philosophy developed. This is often called ‘the Enlightenment'.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
The First World War ended at 11.00 am on 11 November 1918.

explanation.text: The First World War ended at 11.00 am on 11th November 1918 with victory for Britain and its allies.

question.text: Which TWO families contested the Wars of the Roses in the 15th century?

answers.text: A) The House of York and the House of Windsor
B) The House of Windsor and the House of Lancaster
C) The House of Tudor and the House of Windsor
D) The House of York and the House of Lancaster

explanation.text: A civil war known as the Wars of the Roses broke out in 1455 between supporters of the House of Lancaster and the House of York. It ended in 1485 at the Battle of Bosworth Field, when King Richard III of the House of York was killed.

question.text: What was the Beveridge Report of 1942 about?

answers.text: A) How to end the war in Europe
B) How to treat the Germans and Japanese after the war

explanation.text: The Beveridge Report of 1942, called Social Insurance and Allied Services, recommended that the government should find ways of fighting the five 'Giant Evils' of Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor and Idleness and provided the basis of the modern welfare state.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Britain and Germany developed Concorde, a supersonic passenger aircraft.

explanation.text: False - Britain and France developed Concorde, the supersonic passenger aircraft. It first flew in 1969 and began carrying passengers in 1976. Concorde was retired from service in 2003.

question.text: Which of these events changed the powers of the king in 1215?

explanation.text: In 1215, King John was forced by his noblemen to agree to a number of demands. The result was a charter of rights called the Magna Carta (which means the Great Charter). The Magna Carta established the idea that even the king was subject to the law. It protected the rights of the nobility and restricted the king’s power to collect taxes or to make or change laws.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) After the Bill of Rights was declared in 1689, two political groups emerged, the Whigs and the Tories.
B) After the Bill of Rights was declared in 1689, two political groups emerged, the Labour Party and the Greens.

explanation.text:
After the Bill of Rights was declared in 1689,  there were two main groups in Parliament, known
as the Whigs and the Tories. (The modern Conservative Party is
still sometimes referred to as the Tories.)

question.text: For which TWO reasons is Henry VIII remembered?

answers.text: A) Married six times and fought in the Battle of Agincourt
B) Married six times and broke away from the Church of Rome
C) Introduced the game of croquet and married six times
D) Introduced the game of croquet and fought in the Battle of Agincourt

explanation.text: Henry VIII is remembered for breaking away from the Catholic Church of Rome and marrying six times.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
In the 1830s and 1840s a group called the Chartists campaigned for reform to the voting system.

explanation.text: The Chartists campaigned for reform of the voting system. The changes they wanted included elections every year and for all regions to be equal in the electoral system.

question.text: Which festival is celebrated on 31 October?

explanation.text: Halloween is celebrated on 31 October.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Every household with a device that can be used to watch live TV must have a television licence.
B) People who watch TV only on their computers do not need to pay for a television licence.

explanation.text: Everyone in the UK with a TV, computer or other medium which can be used for watching live TV must have a television licence. One licence covers all of the equipment in one home, except when people rent different rooms in a shared house and each has a separate tenancy agreement – those people must each buy a separate licence.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Pantomimes are plays based on fairy stories.

explanation.text: Pantomime plays are a British tradition. Many theatres produce a pantomime at Christmas time. They are based on fairy stories and are light-hearted plays with music and comedy.

question.text: Which TWO are Protestant Christian groups in the UK?

explanation.text: Baptists and Methodists are Protestant Christian groups. Other Protestant groups in the UK include the Church of England, the Church of Scotland, Presbyterians and Quakers.

question.text: For which TWO types of literature is William Shakespeare famous?

explanation.text: William Shakespeare was a famous playwright and sonnet writer.

question.text: What is the name of the UK currency?

explanation.text: The currency in the UK is the pound sterling (symbol £). There are 100 pence in a pound.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Most shops in the UK open seven days a week.
B) All shops in the UK close on Sundays.

explanation.text: Most shops in the UK open seven days a week, although trading hours on Sundays and public holidays are generally reduced.

question.text: Which TWO of the following are Christian festivals celebrated in the UK?

explanation.text: Easter and Christmas are two Christian festivals. Christmas celebrates the birth of Jesus Christ, and Easter marks his death on Good Friday and his rising from the dead on Easter Sunday.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Jane Austen and Charles Dickens were famous novelists.
B) Jane Austen and Charles Dickens were famous painters.

explanation.text: Jane Austen and Charles Dickens were famous novelists. Jane Austen's books include Pride and Prejudice, and Sense and Sensibility. Charles Dickens's novels include Oliver Twist and Great Expectations.

question.text: Which TWO are famous UK landmarks?

answers.text: B) Loch Lomond and Notre Dame
D) Grand Canyon and Loch Lomond

explanation.text: Snowdonia is a national park in North Wales. Loch Lomond is the largest expanse of fresh water in mainland Britain and probably the best-known part of the Trossachs National Park.

question.text: Which event is remembered on 5 November each year?

answers.text: A) The end of the Second World War
C) A plan to blow up the Houses of Parliament in 1605
D) The defeat of the Spanish Armada in 1588

explanation.text: Bonfire Night, 5 November, is an occasion when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays. The origin of this celebration was an event in 1605, when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Gilbert and Sullivan were a comedy double act.
B) Gilbert and Sullivan wrote many comic operas.

explanation.text:
In the 19th century, Gilbert and Sullivan wrote comic operas, often
making fun of popular culture and politics. These operas include
HMS Pinafore, The Pirates of Penzance and The Mikado.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Some people rent land called 'an allotment', where they grow fruit and vegetables.

explanation.text:
A lot of people have gardens at home and will spend their free
time looking after them. Some people rent additional land called ‘an allotment’, where they grow fruit and vegetables.

question.text: Who first built the Tower of London?

explanation.text:
The Tower of London was first built by William the Conqueror
after he became king in 1066.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) There is a yearly sailing race on the River Thames between Oxford and Cambridge universities.
B) There is a yearly rowing race on the River Thames between Oxford and Cambridge universities.

explanation.text: Rowing is a popular sport in the UK, both as a leisure activity and as a competitive sport. There is a popular yearly race on the River Thames between Oxford and Cambridge universities.

question.text: Which TWO of the following are Christian groups?

explanation.text: Roman Catholics and Baptists are Christian groups. Baptists are a Protestant Christian group. Other Protestant groups include the Church of England, Methodists, Presbyterians and Quakers.

question.text: Which is the most popular sport in the UK?

explanation.text: Football is the UK's most popular sport. It has a long history in the UK and the first professional football clubs were formed in the late 19th century.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Proms is an eight-week summer season of classical orchestral music.
B) The Proms is a series of tennis matches held every June in London.

explanation.text: The Proms is an eight-week summer season of orchestral classical music that takes place in various venues, including the Royal Albert Hall in London.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Most people in the UK live in towns and cities.

explanation.text:
Most people live in towns and cities but much of Britain is
still countryside.

question.text: Which TWO of the following are famous British authors?

answers.text: A) Gustav Holst and J K Rowling
B) Sir Steve Redgrave and Sir Arthur Conan Doyle
C) Sir Steve Redgrave and Gustav Holst
D) Sir Arthur Conan Doyle and J K Rowling

explanation.text: Sir Arthur Conan Doyle was a Scottish doctor and writer. He was best known for his stories about Sherlock Holmes, who was one of the first fictional detectives. J K Rowling wrote the Harry Potter series of children’s books, which have enjoyed huge international success. She now writes fiction for adults as well.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
On the 1st of April, people in the UK play jokes on each other until midday.

explanation.text:
April Fool’s Day, 1 April, is a day when people play jokes on each
other until midday. The television and newspapers often have stories that are April Fool jokes.

question.text: Which TWO of the following are linked to football?

answers.text: A) The Premier League and The Open
B) UEFA and the Premier League
D) The Ashes and The Open

explanation.text:
The English Premier League attracts a huge international
audience. Many of the best players in the world play in the Premier League. Many UK teams also compete in competitions such as the UEFA (Union of European Football Associations) Champions League, against other teams from Europe.

question.text: Which TWO commemorations or celebrations are held in November each year?

answers.text: A) Remembrance Day and Bonfire Night
B) Father's Day and Valentine's Day
C) Valentine's Day and Remembrance Day
D) Valentine's Day and Bonfire Night

explanation.text:
Bonfire Night, 5 November, is an occasion when people in Great
Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays. Remembrance Day, 11 November, commemorates those who died fighting for the UK and its allies.

question.text: St David is the patron saint of which country of the UK?

explanation.text: St David is the patron saint of Wales, and St David's Day is celebrated on 1 March each year.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
There are many variations in language in the different parts of the UK.

explanation.text: There are many variations in language in the different parts of the UK. The English language has many accents and dialects. In Wales, many people speak Welsh. In Scotland, Gaelic is spoken in some parts of the Highlands and Islands, and in Northern Ireland some people speak Irish Gaelic.

question.text: Which of these is the name of a novel by Jane Austen?

answers.text: B) Far from the Madding Crowd

explanation.text: Jane Austen was an English novelist. Her books include Pride and Prejudice and Sense and Sensibility. Her novels are concerned with marriage and family relationships. Many have been made into television programmes or films.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Lancelot 'Capability' Brown and Gertrude Jekyll were famous garden designers.
B) Lancelot 'Capability' Brown and Gertrude Jekyll were famous characters in a Sherlock Holmes story.

explanation.text: In the 18th century, Lancelot ‘Capability’ Brown designed the grounds around country houses so that the landscape appeared to be natural, with grass, trees and lakes. Later, Gertrude Jekyll often worked with Sir Edwin Lutyens to design colourful gardens around the houses he designed.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Northern Ireland and Scotland have their own banknotes.

explanation.text: Northern Ireland and Scotland have their own banknotes, which are valid everywhere in the UK.

question.text: What are Beowulf, The Tyger and She Walks in Beauty?

explanation.text: Beowulf is an Anglo-Saxon poem, The Tyger is a poem by William Blake, and She Walks in Beauty is a poem by Lord Byron.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Snowdonia is a national park in Northern Ireland.

explanation.text: Snowdonia is a national park in North Wales. Its most well-known landmark is Snowdon, which is the highest mountain in Wales.

question.text: Which event occurs each year on the third Sunday in June?

explanation.text:
Father’s Day is the third Sunday in June. Children send cards or buy
gifts for their fathers.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Mousetrap is a play that has been running in London's West End since 1952.
B) The Mousetrap is an environmental policy aiming to prevent mice from destroying crops.

explanation.text: The Mousetrap, a murder-mystery play by Dame Agatha Christie, has been running in the West End since 1952.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
UK citizens must practise a Christian religion.

explanation.text: False - everyone has the legal right to choose their religion, or to choose not to practise a religion.

question.text: Which of the following is a traditional food associated with Scotland?

explanation.text: Haggis is a traditional Scottish food. It is a sheep’s stomach stuffed with offal, suet, onions and oatmeal.

question.text: Which TWO of the following are major horse-racing events in the UK?

answers.text: A) The Open Championship and the Scottish Grand National
B) The Open Championship and the Six Nations Championship
C) Scottish Grand National and the Six Nations Championship

question.text: Which TWO religions celebrate Diwali?

explanation.text: Diwali is celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs. It celebrates the victory of good over evil and the gaining of knowledge.

question.text: Where do the Laurence Olivier awards take place?

explanation.text:
The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues
in London. There are a variety of categories, including best director,
best actor and best actress.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Dundee and Aberdeen are cities in Northern Ireland.

explanation.text: Dundee and Aberdeen are cities in Scotland.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Big Ben is a mountain in eastern England

explanation.text:

Ben as well.

question.text: When walking your dog in a public place, what must you ensure?

answers.text: A) That your dog wears a special dog coat
B) That your dog never strays more than 3 metres away from you
C) That your dog does not come into contact with other dogs

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) A famous sailing event is held at Cowes on the Isle of Wight.

question.text: Which TWO events are Christian festivals?

explanation.text: Easter and Christmas are two Christian festivals. Christmas celebrates the birth of Jesus Christ, and Easter marks his death on Good Friday and his rising from the dead on Easter Sunday.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
In the UK, there are now a record number of people aged 85 and over.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text:
A) Halloween is when lovers exchange cards and
gifts.

explanation.text: Halloween is an ancient festival and has its roots in the pagan festival to mark the beginning of winter.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The first professional UK football clubs were formed in the late 19th century

D) Sir Chris Hoy and Lucien Freud

question.text:

The 40 days before Easter are known as Lent.

explanation.text: Lent is a time when Christians take time to reflect and prepare for Easter. Traditionally, people would fast during this period and today many people will give something up, like a favourite food.

question.text: Which TWO of the following are major outdoor music festivals?

explanation.text: St Andrew is the patron saint of Scotland, and is celebrated on 30 November each year.

explanation.text: St George is the patron saint of England, and is celebrated on 23rd of April each year.

answers.text: A) They were all famous British athletes.
B) They all became Prime Minister.

explanation.text: Several British writers, including the novelist Sir William Golding, the poet Seamus Heaney, and the playwright Harold Pinter, have won the Nobel Prize in Literature.

question.text: What celebration takes place each year on 14 February?

explanation.text:
Valentine’s Day, on 14 February, is when lovers exchange cards and
gifts. Sometimes people send anonymous cards to someone they
secretly admire.

question.text: Which of the following UK landmarks is in Northern Ireland?

explanation.text:
Located on the north-east coast of Northern Ireland, the Giant’s
Causeway is a land formation of columns made from volcanic
lava.

answers.text: A) Sir Steve Redgrave is a famous rower who won gold medals in five consecutive Olympic Games.

explanation.text:

Olympians.

B) St David and St Patrick

John Constable (1776–1837) founded the modern police force in England.

question.text: On which date is St Patrick's Day celebrated?

explanation.text: St Patrick, the patron saint of Northern Ireland (and Ireland), has a special day on 17 March.

question.text: Which TWO foods are associated with England?

answers.text: A) Ulster fry and roast beef

explanation.text: Sir Andy Murray is a Scottish tennis player and the first British man to win a singles tennis title in a Grand Slam tournament since 1936.

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Thomas Hardy was a famous author who wrote Far from the Madding Crowd.

question.text: Which TWO of the following are UK landmarks?

B) The Eisteddfodd and the National Trust

question.text: Which of the following is the capital city of Wales?

explanation.text: The capital city of Wales is Cardiff.

question.text:

answers.text: C) Preserving important buildings and places

explanation.text:
Many parts of the countryside and places of interest are kept open
by the National Trust in England, Wales and Northern Ireland and
the National Trust for Scotland. Both are charities that work to
preserve important buildings, coastline and countryside in the
UK.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Rugby was introduced to ancient Britain by Viking invaders.

explanation.text: Rugby originated in England in the early 19th century and is a very popular sport in the UK today.

question.text:

question.text: Which TWO are famous gardens in the UK?

explanation.text: Sissinghurst is in England and Bodnant Garden is in Wales.

question.text:

William Blake, Lord Byron and Robert Browning were all famous golfers.

answers.text: A) Cricket matches can last up to five days.

explanation.text: Football is the UK's most popular sport. It has a long history in the UK, and the English Premier League attracts a huge international audience.

question.text:

The daffodil is the national flower of Wales.

question.text: Which TWO of these novels are by Charles Dickens?

question.text: Which TWO of the following are famous British artists?

question.text:

The money from TV licences is used to pay for the British
Broadcasting Corporation (BBC). Everyone in the UK with a TV, computer or other medium which can be used for watching live TV must have a television licence.

question.text: Which TWO famous London buildings are built in the 19th-century 'gothic' style?

question.text: What type of church is the Church of Scotland?

a Presbyterian Church.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

D) Capability Brown and Vivienne Westwood

Scotland.

question.text:

The flower that is particularly associated with England is the rose.

explanation.text:
The countries that make up the UK
all have flowers which are particularly associated with them and which are sometimes worn on national saints’ days. In England, the flower is the rose.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

question.text:

question.text: Which TWO are famous horse-racing events?

B) The Grand National and Royal Ascot

question.text:

Most people live in the countryside in the UK.

People can see the Crown Jewels at the Tower of London.

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?
