explanation.text: Volunteering is working for good causes without payment. There are many benefits to volunteering, including meeting new people and helping make your community a better place.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Anybody can be asked to serve on a jury, no matter how old they are.

explanation.text: Anyone who is on the electoral register and is aged 18–70 (18–75 in England and Wales) can be asked to serve on a jury.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which TWO of the following are part of the UK government?

answers.text: A) The cabinet and the civil service
B) The civil service and FIFA
C) The National Trust and FIFA
D) The cabinet and the National Trust

explanation.text: There are several different parts of government in the UK, including the cabinet and the civil service.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
In the 1830s and 1840s a group called the Chartists campaigned for reform to the voting system.

explanation.text: The Chartists campaigned for reform of the voting system. The changes they wanted included elections every year and for all regions to be equal in the electoral system.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Once you are aged 17, which TWO vehicles can you learn to drive?

answers.text: B) Motor cycle and fire engine
C) Motor cycle and heavy goods vehicle

explanation.text: In the UK, you must be at least 17 years old to drive a car or motor cycle and you must have a driving licence to drive on public roads.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
The criminal court systems in England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland are identical.

explanation.text: There are some differences between the criminal court systems in different parts of the UK.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: What happens when Members of Parliament (MPs) hold surgeries?

answers.text: A) Local councillors meet their MP to discuss local issues
B) Members of the public meet their MP to discuss issues
C) MPs meet doctors to discuss local health issues
D) MPs meet the press to discuss national issues

explanation.text: Many MPs hold regular local ‘surgeries’, where constituents can go in person to talk about issues that are of concern to them.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Carrying a weapon is an example of a criminal offence.
B) Being in debt is an example of a criminal offence.

explanation.text: Carrying a weapon is one example of a criminal offence. Crimes are usually investigated by the police and punished by the courts.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
If you are a qualifying Commonwealth citizen living in the UK, you can vote in all public elections.

explanation.text: Adult citizens of the UK, and qualifying citizens of the Commonwealth and Ireland who are resident in the UK, can vote in all public elections.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which court would you use to get back money that was owed to you?

explanation.text:
County Courts deal with a wide range of civil disputes. These
include people trying to get back money that is owed to them.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which TWO actions can a judge take if a public body is not respecting someone's legal rights?

answers.text: A) Order the body to pay compensation and close down the public body
B) Place the body's members in prison and close down the public body
C) Order the body to change its practices and order the body to pay compensation
D) Place the body's members in prison and order the body to change its practices

explanation.text: If judges find that a public body is not respecting someone's legal rights, they can order that body to change its practices and/or pay compensation.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which TWO courts deal with minor criminal cases in the UK?

answers.text: A) Justice of the Peace Court and Magistrates' Court
B) Centre Court and Crown Court
C) Justice of the Peace Court and Crown Court
D) Crown Court and Magistrates' Court

explanation.text: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, most minor criminal cases are dealt with in a Magistrates' Court. In Scotland, minor criminal offences go to a Justice of the Peace Court.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: What must police officers do?

explanation.text: Police officers must obey the law. They must not be rude or abusive, make a false statement, misuse their authority, or commit racial discrimination.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: What is the title of the National Anthem of the UK?

explanation.text: The National Anthem of the UK is 'God Save the King'. It is played at important national occasions and at events attended by the King or the Royal Family.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Who do some local councils appoint as a ceremonial leader?

answers.text: B) A member of the Royal Family

explanation.text: Many local authorities appoint a mayor, who is the ceremonial leader of the council. (In some towns, a mayor is elected to be the effective leader of the administration.)

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) There are a few Members of Parliament who do not represent any of the main political parties.
B) All Members of Parliament have to belong to a political party.

explanation.text: There are a few Members of Parliament (MPs) who do not represent any of the main political parties. They are called 'independents'.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
The Home Secretary is the government minister responsible for managing relationships with foreign countries.

explanation.text: The Home Secretary is the government minister responsible for crime, policing and immigration. The Foreign Secretary is the government minister responsible for managing relationships with foreign countries.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: What is one of the roles of school governors?

answers.text: A) Setting the strategic direction of the school
C) Giving teachers ideas for lesson plans
D) Serving food and drink in the canteen

explanation.text: School governors have an important part to play in raising school standards. This includes setting the strategic direction of the school.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: What sort of cases do Crown Courts and Sheriff Courts deal with?

explanation.text: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, serious offences are tried in front of a judge and jury in a Crown Court. In Scotland, serious cases are heard in a Sheriff Court with either a sheriff or a sheriff with a jury.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: What is the youngest age at which you can be asked to serve on a jury?

explanation.text: Anyone who is on the electoral register and is aged 18–70 (18–75 in England and Wales) can be asked to serve on a jury.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Proceedings in Parliament cannot be reported in the press.
B) Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television.

explanation.text: Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television and published in official reports called Hansard. Most people get information about political issues and events from newspapers (often called ‘the press’), television, radio and the internet.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Decisions on government policies are made by the monarch.
B) Decisions on government policies are made by the Prime Minister and cabinet.

explanation.text: The monarch has regular meetings with the Prime Minister, and can advise, warn and encourage, but the decisions on government policies are made by the Prime Minister and cabinet.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Pressure and lobby groups try to influence government policy.

explanation.text: Pressure and lobby groups are organisations which try to influence government policy. They play an important role in politics.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The civil service largely consists of political appointees.
B) The civil service is politically neutral.

explanation.text:
Civil servants support the government in developing and
implementing its policies. They are chosen on merit and are politically neutral – they are not political appointees.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
An example of a civil law case is when you have purchased a faulty item and made a legal complaint.

explanation.text: Civil law is used to settle disputes between individuals or groups.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: How is the Speaker of the House of Commons chosen?

explanation.text: The Speaker is chosen by other Members of Parliament (MPs) in a secret ballot. The Speaker keeps order during political debates to make sure the rules are followed.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Police and Crime Commissioners (PCCs) are appointed through a public election.
B) Police and Crime Commissioners (PCCs) are appointed by the local council.

explanation.text: The public in England and Wales elect Police and Crime Commissioners (PCCs). The first elections for PCCs were held in November 2012.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) It is free to visit the Houses of Parliament to listen to debates.
B) It costs £15 per person to visit the Houses of Parliament to listen to debates.

explanation.text:
The public can listen to debates from public galleries in both the House of Commons and the
House of Lords. Entrance is free. You can write to your local MP in advance to ask for tickets or you can queue on the day at the public entrance.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following do you need to do in order to get a full driving licence?

answers.text: B) Buy a car or van

explanation.text: To get a full UK driving licence, you must pass a driving test, which tests both your knowledge and your practical skills.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following is dealt with under civil law?

explanation.text: Debt is covered by civil law. People might be taken to court if they owe money to someone.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) The Chancellor of the Exchequer is responsible for crime, policing and immigration.
B) The Chancellor of the Exchequer is responsible for the economy.

explanation.text: The Chancellor of the Exchequer is the cabinet minister responsible for the economy.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: What happens when a Member of Parliament (MP) dies or resigns?

answers.text: A) The post remains vacant until the next General Election.
B) Their party chooses someone to fill the post until the next General Election.
C) A by-election is held to replace the MP.
D) A neighbouring MP looks after the constituency.

explanation.text: If an MP dies or resigns, there will be a fresh election, called a by-election, in his or her constituency.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
The House of Lords always acts as the government wishes.

explanation.text: The House of Lords is normally more independent of the government than the House of Commons.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
A General Election occurs every eight years.

explanation.text: A General Election is held at least every five years.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: When you arrive at a polling station, the staff will ask for which of the following:

answers.text: B) Name, address and photo ID.
C) Life in the UK test certificate.

explanation.text: People vote in elections at places called polling stations, or polling places in Scotland. When you arrive at the polling station or place, the staff will ask for your name, address and photo ID. You can check that you have the right photo ID at www.gov.uk/how-to-vote/photo-id-youll-need. You will then get your ballot paper, which you take to a polling booth to fill in privately.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) All Acts of Parliament are made in the monarch's name.
B) All Acts of Parliament are made in the Prime Minister's name.

explanation.text: The monarch is the head of state of the UK. All Acts of Parliament are made in the monarch's name.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which parts of the United Kingdom have devolved governments?

answers.text: B) Wales, England and Northern Ireland
D) Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland

explanation.text: Some powers have been devolved from central government to give people in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland more control over matters that directly affect them.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) You should only put your refuse bins in communal areas if they are due to be collected
B) You should put your refuse bins in communal areas every day

explanation.text: You should only put your refuse bags and bins on the street or in communal areas if they are due to be collected.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) In a Crown Court case, the judge decides the penalty when someone is found guilty.
B) In a Crown Court case, the jury decides the penalty when someone is found guilty.

explanation.text: If the jury in a Crown Court case finds the defendant guilty, the judge decides on the penalty.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
The Scottish Parliament sits in Edinburgh.

explanation.text:
The Scottish Parliament was formed in 1999. It sits in Edinburgh, the
capital city of Scotland.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

answers.text: A) Local elections are normally held in May.
B) Local elections are normally held in March.

explanation.text: For most local authorities, local elections for councillors are held in May every year.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Hereditary peers have the automatic right to attend the House of Lords.

explanation.text: Since 1999, hereditary peers have lost the automatic right to attend the House of Lords. They now elect a few of their number to represent them.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text:
Is the statement below TRUE or FALSE?
Parents can often help at schools by supporting classroom activities or listening to children read.

explanation.text: If you have children, there are many ways in which you can help at their schools. Parents can often help in classrooms, by supporting activities or listening to children read.

topic: The UK government, the law and your role

question.text: Which TWO of the following types of case are held in County Courts?

answers.text: B) Murder and minor criminal offences
C) Minor criminal offences and breaches of contract
D) Divorce and breaches of contract

explanation.text: County Courts deal with a wide range of civil disputes, including divorce and other family matters, and breaches of contract.
